comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on arXiv's recent independence from Cornell and concerns about its future direction. Commenters express skepticism about potential commercialization, governance changes, and the impact on arXiv's role as an open-access preprint server.

comments.author: frankling_
comments.text:
The recent announcement to reject review articles and position papers already smelled like a shift towards a more "opinionated" stance, and this move smells worse.

The vacuum that arXiv originally filled was one of a glorified PDF hosting service with just enough of a reputation to allow some preprints to be cited in a formally published paper, and with just enough moderation to not devolve into spam and chaos. It has also been instrumental in pushing publishers towards open access (i.e., to finally give up).

Unfortunately, over the years, arXiv has become something like a "venue" in its own right, particularly in ML, with some decently cited papers never formally published and "preprints" being cited left and right. Consider the impression you get when seeing a reference to an arXiv preprint vs. a link to an author's institutional website.

In my view, arXiv fulfills its function better the less power it has as an institution, and I thus have exactly zero trust that the split from Cornell is driven by that function. We've seen the kind of appeasement prose from their statement and FAQ [1] countless times before, and it's now time for the usual routine of snapshotting the site to watch the inevitable amendments to the mission statement.

"What positive changes should users expect to see?" - I guess the negative ones we'll have to see for ourselves.

[1] https://tech.cornell.edu/arxiv/
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced historical perspective on arXiv's evolution and expresses justified concerns about institutional changes, supported by observations of prior policy shifts.

comments.author: dataflow
comments.text:
This sounds terrible. Of course there's a huge risk of it becoming made for-profit. It almost makes you wonder if the academic publishers are behind this push somehow.

Could they not have made it into some legal structure that puts universities at the top? Say, with a bunch of universities owning shares that comprise the entirety of the ownership of arXiv, but that would allow arXiv to independently raise funds?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about potential commercialization and loss of independence, though the suggestion of publisher involvement is speculative without evidence.

comments.author: Aerolfos
comments.text:
And they hired a LinkedIn business idiot to run the new organization - so the aim is for an infinite growth tech startup in terms of governance, despite the technical legal status of non-profit. It shows in the language they use in the announcement, too ("improved financial viability in the long run")

OpenAI shows exactly how well that works and what that kind of governance does to a company and to its support of science and the commons.

TL;DR, it's fucked.
comments.analysis: Highlights governance changes and potential mission drift; while the tone is harsh, concerns about startup-like growth models in non-profits are reasonable.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
"Recently arXiv’s growth has accelerated. Since 2022, it has expanded its staff to 27, in large part to deal with a 50% increase in submitted manuscripts."

I am wary of that. IMO the business model is damaged therein. You can say in 2022 we had 27; bankrupt in 2030.
comments.analysis: Observes factual growth metrics and expresses caution about sustainability, a reasonable point given organizational scaling challenges.

comments.author: OutOfHere
comments.text: With 300K for the CEO, its enshittification will commence imminently. It will now serve to maximize revenue. Just wait and watch while they issue a premium membership, payment requirements for authors, and other revenue generators to please their investors.
comments.analysis: Highly speculative and alarmist without evidence; the claim about CEO salary and immediate monetization plans lacks substantiation.

comments.author: Garlef
comments.text:
Maybe they should implement a graph based trust system:

You need your favourite academic gatekeeper (= thesis advisor) to vouch for you in order to be allowed to upload.

Then AI slop gets flagged and the shame spreads through the graph. And flaggings need to have evidence attached that can again be flagged.
comments.analysis: Proposes an innovative moderation system to maintain quality, showing constructive thinking though implementation feasibility is unclear.

comments.author: psalminen
comments.text: I might be missing something, but I still don't get the why. I don't see any "problem" that needs to be solved.
comments.analysis: Expresses genuine confusion about the rationale behind the change, reflecting a common user perspective but lacking deeper insight.

comments.author: davnicwil
comments.text:
Very unrelated to the article, but I think 'arXiv' as a brand is bad, and really detrimental to what the institution aims to accomplish.

That is, it's not readily parseable, it really gives an insider term vibe - like this isn't for you if you don't already know what it means or how you should read or say it. It sort of reminds me of the overuse of latin and latinate terms generally in the old professions and, well, the academy.

Just always struck me as being somewhat at odds with the goal.
comments.analysis: Raises a branding issue unrelated to the independence topic; while thoughtful, it does not contribute meaningfully to the main discussion.

comments.author: Peteragain
comments.text: .. and soon to be dependent on US military funding? Controlled by someone who has run-ins with universities? This'll end in tears.
comments.analysis: Speculative conspiracy-like comment without evidence, contributing fear but lacking factual basis.

comments.author: asimpleusecase
comments.text: I wonder if there are plans to licence the content for AI training
comments.analysis: Poses a relevant question about future content licensing, reflecting a topical concern in AI and publishing.

comments.author: tornikeo
comments.text: Now the question is, will arxiv wage a decade long bloody war with Cornell, using heavy infantry (PhD students), archers (reviewers) and field artillery (AI slop papers), or will the independence be mostly peaceful? Only time can tell.
comments.analysis: Uses humorous military metaphor to describe potential conflict, but adds little substantive analysis.

comments.author: adamnemecek
comments.text: Good call, ArXiv seems like one of the most important institutions out there right now.
comments.analysis: Expresses positive sentiment but does not provide analysis or new information.
